Requirement: The platform must provide a unified application layer covering asset issuance, compliance enforcement, custody integration, settlement, and servicing as integrated modules rather than standalone tools.
The platform must implement a digital twin model where each tokenized asset is represented as an on-chain entity with full lifecycle state tracking.
The platform must support multi-tenant deployments with strict logical isolation of tenant data, configuration, roles, and on-chain governance boundaries.
The platform must support horizontal scaling of API services, indexer workers, and observability components independently via Kubernetes-based orchestration.
The platform must expose a comprehensive REST API with OpenAPI specification, supporting both synchronous and asynchronous (HTTP 202) transaction modes.
The platform must use a durable execution engine that guarantees workflow completion through infrastructure failures and process restarts.
The platform must include a blockchain event indexer capable of zero-downtime reindexing, reorg detection, and projection of on-chain events into queryable read models.
The platform must implement ERC-3643 (T-REX) as the native regulated token standard, with UUPS proxy upgradeability for all major contracts.
The platform must support tokenization of at minimum: bonds, equities, funds, stablecoins, deposits, real estate, and precious metals as distinct asset classes with purpose-built lifecycle logic.

The platform must offer a composable token architecture allowing institutions to assemble custom asset types from pluggable features (e.g., fees, yield, voting, maturity) without writing new smart contracts.
The platform must enforce a deterministic issuance workflow with paused-by-default behavior, requiring explicit permission grants and compliance configuration before tokens become transferable.
The platform must support minting, burning, transferring, forced-transfer (for recovery), and batch operations, all routed through an auditable async transaction pipeline.
The platform must automate corporate actions including coupon payments, dividend distribution, maturity handling, and redemption processing programmatically.
The platform must support pause/unpause at asset level and freeze/unfreeze at individual holder level, with full audit trail for each state transition.
The platform must support primary token sales with configurable presale/public phases, multi-currency acceptance, per-investor purchase limits, and vesting schedules.
The platform must support smart contract upgrades via proxy patterns without requiring redeployment or loss of on-chain state.
Describe the full catalog of pluggable token features available (e.g., AUM fees, transaction fees, collateral management, maturity redemption, voting power, historical balances).
The platform must provide a guided asset design interface with multi-step validation, configurable compliance rules, and class-specific parameters for non-technical operators.
The platform must enforce compliance ex-ante — validating every transfer against eligibility rules, identity claims, and jurisdictional constraints before execution on-chain.

The platform must provide at minimum 15 configurable compliance module types covering eligibility, restrictions, transfer controls, issuance/supply limits, time-based rules, and settlement/collateral.

The platform must implement on-chain verifiable investor identities with claim-based verification for KYC/KYB credentials, accreditation status, and jurisdictional eligibility, reusable across all assets.
The platform must support integration with third-party KYC/KYB providers and provide a multi-version KYC profile system with explicit review workflow (draft → under_review → approved/rejected).
The platform must provide pre-configured compliance module sets for at least: MiCA (EU), Reg D/S/CF (US), MAS (Singapore), FCA (UK), and JFSA (Japan) frameworks.
The platform must support a multi-tier trusted issuers registry with at minimum subject-scoped, system-scoped, and global resolution for trusted credential issuers.
The platform must support a multi-tier compliance interface hierarchy enabling incremental migration of individual tokens to new compliance standards without platform-wide cutover.
The platform must support both country allow-list and country block-list compliance modules configurable per asset.
The platform must support configurable holding period (time-lock) enforcement with exemption expressions for specific investor categories or transaction types.
The platform must support transfer approval workflows where designated compliance officers can approve or reject transfers that require manual review.
The platform must provide a compliance simulation/pre-check capability that validates whether a proposed transfer would pass all compliance rules before submission.
The platform must provide a governed identity recovery process for wallet loss or compromise with deterministic phase tracking and operator oversight.
The platform must support atomic Delivery-versus-Payment (DvP) settlement where both asset and cash legs complete together or both revert.
The platform must support Exchange-versus-Payment (XvP) settlement with full lifecycle management including approval, hashlock, cancellation vote logic, and expiry-withdrawal.
The platform must support a bring-your-own-custodian model with current production integrations for at least two institutional custody providers.
List all custody providers currently integrated and describe the depth of each integration (wallet CRUD, transaction signing, approval workflows, provider-native broadcast).
The platform must provide key management with support for multiple secrets backends (e.g., local encrypted, HashiCorp Vault, cloud provider secret managers).
The platform must enforce multi-factor verification gates (e.g., PIN, TOTP, backup codes) for privileged transaction signing operations.
The platform must support delegating nonce allocation, gas handling, signing, and broadcast to the custody provider when supported, while retaining admission control and workflow state.
Describe how the platform ensures settlement finality and handles settlement failures, timeouts, or counterparty non-performance.
The platform must support integration with institutional payment rails (SWIFT, SEPA, RTGS) via ISO 20022 message standards for fiat settlement legs.
The platform must support custody vault operations for secure asset holding with governed access controls and audit trails.

The platform must enforce a hardened two-endpoint authentication architecture separating session-based (UI) and API-key-based (programmatic) access, with API keys explicitly blocked from session endpoints.
The platform must enforce HTTP-method-based scope control on API keys (read-only for GET, read-write for mutations) to prevent privilege escalation.
All API communications must be encrypted via TLS. Blockchain node communication must use HTTPS.
The platform must support encryption of sensitive data at rest using industry-standard encryption (AES-256 or equivalent) across all storage backends.
The platform must maintain a comprehensive, immutable audit trail covering all asset operations, compliance decisions, identity events, and role changes with full event context.
The platform must provide structured error codes with metadata (severity, audience, retryable flag, suggested action) for operational forensics on failed transactions.
Provide evidence of third-party penetration testing conducted on the platform within the last 12 months, including scope, testing firm, and summary of findings.
The platform must enforce schema-based input validation across all API endpoints with protection against SQL injection, XSS, and path traversal attacks.
The platform must implement automated checks that reject deployment of development-only credentials, default signing keys, and insecure defaults in production environments.
List all security certifications held by the organization (e.g., ISO 27001, SOC 2 Type II) and their current validity dates.
The platform must implement role-based access control (RBAC) with at minimum 5 defined roles enforced both on-chain (smart contracts) and off-chain (API middleware).
The platform must separate system-level permissions (identity management, compliance configuration) from token-level permissions (minting, transferring, pausing) to enable granular authorization.
The platform must support users belonging to multiple organizations with different roles in each, with membership-gated active-organization persistence.
The platform must support segregation of duties with maker-checker approval workflows for privileged operations at both platform and custody layers.
The platform must enforce invitation-gated tenant onboarding with explicit membership boundaries to prevent unauthorized access to tenant resources.
The platform must support per-asset role assignments allowing different operators to manage different tokenized assets within the same tenant.
The platform must expose full CRUD and mutation access to all platform capabilities via a documented REST API with OpenAPI specification.
The platform must provide a typed SDK (e.g., TypeScript) with contract-bound client, blockchain-aware serializers, and structured error handling for integration development.
The platform must provide a comprehensive CLI with at minimum 200 commands covering system administration, token lifecycle, identity/KYC, compliance, and monitoring.
The platform must support configurable event-driven webhook notifications for downstream system integration (e.g., settlement events, compliance decisions, lifecycle state changes).
All mutation operations must support idempotency keys to enable safe retry from external systems without duplicate transaction risk.
Describe the available patterns and reference architectures for integrating the platform with core banking systems.
The platform must support governed registration and lifecycle management of external tokens (e.g., third-party stablecoins used for settlement) with tenant-scoped isolation.
The platform must be integration-ready for ISO 20022 messaging standards for connectivity with SWIFT, SEPA, and RTGS payment systems.
The platform must ship with a three-pillar observability stack covering metrics, structured logs, and distributed tracing.
The platform must include pre-built operational dashboards covering operations overview, transaction monitoring, compliance activity, and security events.
The platform must provide configurable alert thresholds for error rate spikes, latency degradation, chain connectivity issues, and settlement failures with structured notification delivery.
The platform must provide end-to-end transaction tracing across all pipeline phases (submit → sign → broadcast → confirm) with detailed sub-status classification for failure diagnostics.
The platform must provide automated blockchain network health monitoring with threshold-based classification, historical timeline data, and live streaming capability.
The platform must allow operators to requeue failed transactions from dead-letter state for retry without requiring manual re-submission.
The platform must include documented operational runbooks covering platform setup, asset operations, identity management, monitoring, and recovery procedures.
The platform must expose operational health data, monitoring, and administrative functions through CLI commands for terminal-based and scripted operations.
The platform must support deployment on at least AWS, GCP, and Azure cloud environments with institution-controlled cloud accounts and data locations.
The platform must support full on-premises deployment on standard Kubernetes distributions and Red Hat OpenShift within the institution's data center.
The platform must support hybrid deployment models with production on-premises and non-production in cloud, or any combination.
The platform must support high availability through Kubernetes-native resilience (pod auto-restart, node failover, rolling updates) and durable execution guarantees.
Describe the platform's disaster recovery capabilities including RPO/RTO targets, backup mechanisms, and data reconstruction approach.
The platform must support data residency requirements by allowing institutions to control where all off-chain data (user profiles, KYC data, configuration) is physically stored.
State the maximum sustained transaction throughput (transactions per second) the platform can process under institutional production conditions.
State the typical and 99th percentile API response latencies for common operations (token transfer, compliance check, balance query).
State the maximum number of concurrent users the platform has been tested to support without degradation.
Describe any load testing and stress testing performed on the platform, including methodology, tools, and results.
Describe the indexer's performance characteristics including event processing rate, query response times, and behavior during zero-downtime reindexing.
Describe how the platform supports compliance with the EU Markets in Crypto-Assets Regulation (MiCA) including token classification, whitepaper requirements, and reserve monitoring.
Describe how the platform supports MiFID II requirements for tokenized securities including investor categorization, suitability, and transaction reporting.
Describe how the platform supports compliance with the UAE Virtual Assets Regulatory Authority (VARA) framework.
Describe how the platform handles personal data in compliance with GDPR and equivalent data protection regulations, including data minimization, right to erasure, and consent management.
The platform must provide exportable audit evidence (audit trails, compliance check records, identity verification evidence) in formats suitable for regulatory examination.
Describe the platform licensing model including whether pricing is based on platform access, per-transaction fees, per-asset fees, or other metrics.
Describe available pricing tiers and what capabilities, environments, and support levels are included in each tier.
State the platform uptime SLA commitment (e.g., 99.9%, 99.95%, 99.99%) and describe the service credit mechanism for SLA breaches.
Describe standard contract term lengths, renewal terms, and early termination provisions.
Does the vendor offer source code escrow arrangements for business continuity assurance?
Describe available support tiers including coverage hours, communication channels, and named resource availability for each tier.
State guaranteed response and resolution times for each severity level (P1 Critical through P4 General inquiry) per support tier.
Describe the training program including available tracks (e.g., administrator, developer, end-user), delivery methods, and included documentation.
State the typical implementation timeline from contract signing to production go-live, broken down by phase, for a standard deployment.
Describe the implementation methodology including phases, deliverables per phase, resource requirements from both vendor and client, and governance structure.
Describe the platform's reporting capabilities including pre-built analytics views, statistical reporting, and data export formats (CSV, JSON) available for institutional reporting.

The platform must support bulk data export of all audit trails, transaction history, compliance records, and identity events in machine-readable formats for integration with institutional data warehouses.
